g.data-narration: Welcome to thresholding, the foundation of image segmentation in computer vision.
Segmentation partitions an image into meaningful regions, like separating objects from background.
g.text: Essential first step in image analysis

g.data-narration: A grayscale image is a 2D function f(x,y) where values range from 0 (black) to 255 (white).
g.text: Each pixel has a single intensity

g.data-narration: Thresholding compares each pixel intensity to threshold T. If greater, pixel becomes white (1), else black (0).
A histogram plots intensity frequency. It guides threshold selection by showing intensity distribution.
g.text: Graph showing frequency of each intensity level (0-255)

g.data-narration: Bimodal histograms have two peaks - one for background, one for object. The valley is the optimal threshold.
g.text: Set T in the valley for optimal

g.data-narration: The iterative threshold algorithm finds optimal T by repeatedly segmenting and computing group means.
g.text: Iterative method to find optimal threshold T automatically

g.data-narration: Initialize T to midpoint (128), then segment image into two groups: pixels above and below threshold.
Compute mean intensity of each group, then calculate new threshold as average of these means.
g.text: Calculate average intensity of pixels in each group:

g.data-narration: Check if new threshold differs from old by less than delta (small value). If yes, stop; else iterate.
g.text: Determine if algorithm should continue or terminate:

g.data-narration: Let's walk through an example with object avg=200 and background avg=50. Starting T=128, algorithm converges to 125.
g.text: Object pixels: avg=200 | Background pixels: avg=50

g.data-narration: Global thresholding fails with non-uniform illumination causing poor histogram separation. This requires adaptive methods.
Key points: thresholding converts grayscale to binary using threshold T, works best with bimodal histograms, uses iterative algorithm.
